title: Civilizations
author: Laurent Binet
body:
Started: 2026-03-30
Finished: 2026-04-03
#bookclub

This book is a work of speculative historical fiction. Imagining a world where it was the Inca and the Aztecs that invaded and conquered Europe rather than Spain, Portugal and Britain who took the Americas.

Binet deploys irony with a heavy hand. It is Atahualpa who captures the Spanish King and Holy Roman Emperor rather than Pizarro capturing Atahualpa. The tactics that Atahualpa used in *Civilizations* is exactly the same as those used by the conquistadors in Peru. The accidental death of Charles also mirrors the real events in Peru.

Binet also critiques Christianity. His portrayal of Luther is extremely accurate and the intolerances of the civil and religious environment of the 16th century are well drawn and accurate. That said his portrayal of the Atahualpa and the cult of the Sun as benign and enlightened takes a great deal of latitude. In the land of the Four Quarters all non-nobles held no property, wore the same clothes and had very little freedom. This is not explored or even discussed in the novel. This uncritical view of the Inca civilisation is potentially sentimental. This may be a deliberate choice. The chronicler is culturally Inca - are they lionising their own culture and is this a subtle way for Binet to critique the practice of history as a whole: "history is written by the victor". If so I feel this is too subtle. The lack of contradiction makes me feel it is more likely Binet has romanticized the culture of the Inca. It is easy to fall into this trap when considering the cultures destroyed and the horrors inflicted by Pizzarro and the conquistadors in general. Binet does not give the Aztecs the same treatment. Not surprising given their love of human sacrifice. That is so clearly beyond the pale for even the most committed moral relativist.

The conceit used of a chronicler recording the events of the conquest of the Europe worked well. However, the first few chapters covering the events of arrival of the Danes felt a very different style. Binet seemed to drift into the voice of a chronicler later. This gave the novel a shifting voice that felt a little abrupt to me. I was confused, at first, if I was following different voices but of course I was not. This took me out of the immersion.

I saw this as an enjoyable historical fantasy more akin to Robert Harris' _Fatherland_ than a serious work of literature. I feel this novel was an indulgence of Binet's own cultural prejudices than an attempt to really consider the impact of collonialization. This sits in contrast to _Noughts & Crosses_ by Malorie Blackman which critically explored the nature and origin of contemporary racial prejudice and discrimination.

title: Ship of Magic
author: Robin Hobb
body:
Started: 2026-03-08
Finished: 2026-03-29

Another engaging and thrilling story from the world of the Elderlings. *Ship of Magic* demonstrates Hobb's versatility as a writer. The use of the first person in *The Farseer Trilogy* is suited to the claustrophobic world of court politics and personal sacrifice. In *Ship of Magic*, Hobb needs to broaden her readers' horizon. A first-person perspective would not have worked on the wide open seas. The multiple perspectives used give a sense of scale and grandeur to Hobb's world that was implied in *Farseer* but are drawn vividly in the world of Althea and Vivacia.

Hobb sows doubt in her readers' mind, reflecting the crushing loss of confidence that Althea feels. At first, we are made to doubt Althea's perspective. Is perhaps Kyle's view of Althea correct? Is she just a spoilt daddy's girl with no real skill or understanding? It seems plausible, after all. But as we see Kyle's need for control become more than a man doing what is necessary to save his family fortune—becoming something insidious, dominating, and aggressive—we understand that Althea was right. Hobb takes her reader on a parallel journey of doubt to Althea.

Wintrow and Althea are opposite, but both are used by Hobb to show different ways to self-actualisation and mastery of self. Althea loves the sea and does all she can to become a good sailor—to prove to herself, and to Kyle, that she is more than a captain's daughter. Wintrow has a more complex journey of selfhood. He must understand who he is and what his purpose in life is. Is he a boy, a man, a priest, or a sailor? What is right and what is wrong? Hobb uses Wintrow to explore morality. Where Althea acts, Wintrow thinks.

The liveships are an interesting concept, tied to the idea of sentience, selfhood, and freedom. Are they a tool owned by a family or a member of that family? Pets or equals? Slaves or willing brothers and sisters? This is never resolved, and I see this becoming an important theme in the remainder of the *Liveship Traders*.

It took almost a month to read *Ship of Magic*, not from a lack of enjoyment but due to distraction. Life simply got in the way. That meant I had time to linger and digest Hobb's world rather than devouring it; I savored it. Hobb is well on the way to becoming one of my favorite writers. Her imagination and ability to weave character, philosophy, morality, and ambiguity all together set her apart.

title: The Left Hand of Darkness
author: Ursula K. Le Guin
body:
Started: 2026-03-01
Finished: 2026-03-08
#bookclub

The Left Hand of Darkness is an interesting book. It uses a science fiction premise as a means to conduct a thought experiment — what if gender wasn’t the organizing principle of society? It succeeds as a critique of gender roles. Ai, the Envoy, only superficially examines his own latent misogyny. His fundamental assumptions about masculinity and strength, and femininity (effeminacy) as weakness, are woven through the narrative. His failure to confront this makes the message more poignant, though on a casual read this nuance could easily be lost.

Le Guin critiques masculinity acidly — through the lens of physicality and pride.

> He, after all, had no standards of manliness, of virility, to complicate his pride.

The use of pronouns and their associations is important; the use of “she” and “he” feels deliberate. Ai conflates femininity (“effeminacy”) with weakness throughout, providing a view of Ai’s ingrained chauvinism. Can he not conceive of a person as a “she” without lowering them in his eyes?

> ...to my eyes effeminate—not in the sense of delicacy, etc., but in just the opposite sense: a gross, bland fleshiness, a bovinity without point or edge.

The image of femininity as cow-like is both horrible and resonant. Cows bear calves, they nourish us with their milk, they are stolid, perhaps stupid, and are chattel. Is this how Ai sees all women? Ai tells us that he has never really thought about women and their perspective.

> “Tell me, how does the other sex of your race differ from yours?”
> “...I never thought of that,”
> “...In a sense, women are more alien to me than you are.”

He has, and continues to be, wilfully blind to the female — not rejecting it outright, but failing to conceptualise or consider a feminine perspective, dismissing it as beneath contempt. The fact that this view is hidden, latent in Ai's psyche, is a real achievement of Le Guin. A lesser writer would have made this obvious. The damning thing is that Ai is otherwise a great and model human being. Ai's prejudice isn't monstrous; it's horrific for being ordinary.

This was, for me, the most evocative and resonant sentence in the novel. It describes the privilege of masculinity and the horror (that I share) of losing it:

> One is respected and judged only as a human being. It is an appalling experience.

Here Le Guin hits the nail on the head. To be stripped of the societal protection of masculinity and be judged only by the content of your character is deeply uncomfortable. It forces one to really ask what it means to be human and where one's “worth” as a human being is derived.

I found Le Guin's introduction the most interesting aspect of the novel. Her discussions on truth, analogy, and the purpose of sci-fi, literature, and art were the most intellectually engaging aspects for me.

> and then when they [writers of fiction] are done writing down this pack of lies, they say, There! That’s the truth!

and later,

> Distrust everything I say. I am telling the truth. The only truth I can understand or express is, logically defined, a lie. Psychologically defined, a symbol. Aesthetically defined, a metaphor.

This gets very close to Hilary Mantel's justification, in her 2017 Reith Lectures, of the truth in historical fiction. By inhabiting another person we come to empathise in a way with the other that tells a greater truth.

In this vein Le Guin writes:

> In fact, while we read a novel, we are insane — bonkers. We believe in the existence of people who aren’t there, we hear their voices, we watch the battle of Borodino with them, we may even become Napoleon. Sanity returns (in most cases) when the book is closed.

She sums up the whole beauty of reading fiction — the “double speak” that one most willingly engages in — beautifully.

> In reading a novel, any novel, we have to know perfectly well that the whole thing is nonsense, and then, while reading, believe every word of it.

And finally this most important point:

> The novelist says in words what cannot be said in words.

This introduction set my expectations for The Left Hand of Darkness very high indeed. My expectation of greater internal conflict in Ai was a result of wanting a crisis of conscience that would reveal to Ai that his gendered view of the world has blinded him. I wanted him struck dumb with the realisation.

But on reflection I see Le Guin's subtlety. He is able to empathise with one person (Estraven) and even come to love them. But he can't see females of his own people with clear and unbiased eyes. He is locked forever to repeat the mistake of his culture — failing to judge all as simply human beings.

title: Assassin's Quest
author: Robin Hobb
body:
Started: 2026-02-21
Finished: 2026-02-28

I could not put this down. I read and read — pushing myself well past bedtimes and ignoring almost everything else. I felt the world drawn by Hobb viscerally, and I felt the pain, hate, and love that swim through this book like the current of a pulling river. The frustration of love denied, the acceptance of loss, and the hope for the future.

Hobb's world is complex not just in terms of the bright and full landscape she has built, but in the characters — their motivations and their relationships to one another. Regal is a spoilt child but desperately afraid. Hobb shows that the Red Ships are not mindlessly seeking violence, but continuing a cycle of violence that the Six Duchies, in their own desperation, began. Hobb tackles sexual politics with the Fool and the conflict between themself and Starling with ease. The Fool is himself — and this is the most painful lesson of all. Where the Fool is irrepressibly himself, believing in and living his quest, Fitz is denied his true self — as a loving father and husband — his life is not and never has been his own. The Fool and Fitz can be seen as a literal representation of the double-edged sword of prophecy — the Fool gets what he wants; Fitz is always denied his heart’s desire.

There is also the contrast and tension between the Wit and the Skill. One can read a class dynamic into this as well. The Wit represents a power open to all (all, in some way, have it), and because it is open to all it is repressed and stigmatized by the powerful. It is a power of love, of building. The Skill is the preserve of the Farseers and the nobility; as such, its practice is associated with high status and jealously protected. It is a thing of bright power, able to kill and to inflict dominion on others. Where the Skill is a power of domination, the Wit is a power of bonding and joining — it creates empathy. In Fitz this contrast is most clear. He is controlled and compelled by Verity through the Skill, and loved and nurtured by Nighteyes via the Wit.

The Dragons, or Elderlings, are an interesting creation — monsters, in a way. Requiring the complete sacrifice of identity and life to be born, the complete subjugation of self to power. In a way it is fitting that a king do this for his people, whom he subjugates. There is also the Stone Garden of slumbering beasts desperate to feed and consume life. Are they any different from any other carnivore? Or are they something more sinister? That is a question I cannot yet answer.

Hobb has created a rich world full of realised characters that live, breathe, love, hate, and stink. In its scale it also feels intimate and human. She does this with a gesture, a sigh, the smell of a worn shirt, and the funk of a forest. I can see why the Realm of the Elderlings is loved.

title: The Mercy of Gods
author: James S. A. Corey
body:
Started: 2026-02-16
Finished: 2026-02-21

What fun. This is a very different premise and world to that of the Expanse. I was frustrated in the first half of the novel finding the cycles of ever worsening suffering bleak and repetative than engaging and enjoyable. In the lattter half I found that the first half was a neccessary crucible for the world and the Corrax that Corey was building.

Dafyd moves through three prisons throughout this novel. The first is the hierarchical world of the academy of his home. He is a lab assistant with unfullfiled ambition, trying and failing not to rely on the patronage of his succesfull and powerful aunt. Then there is the stinking organge hell of the transport ship and finally the "World Palace" of the Corrax. These three prisons represent the three phases of Dafyd's awareness. From disengaged academic to reluctant leader and naisent hope of humanity.

Corey's Corrax are utterly alien, capricious and cruel. But Corey does not fall into the trap of painting them as evil one dimensional monsters. He allows them to have their own motivations and morallity summed up in the phrase "what is, is". The Corrax dominate through strength because that is the nature of the universe, of evolution, of life. How can it be wrong?

I admired the intense psychology of the work. The pressure cooker that is created and the new, at least for me, merger of a prison story and science fiction world. The wider universe and the great confict anre more than hinted at and we are given enough to be curious about what comes next. But the novel can be read as a standalone volume - this I appreciate. It demonstrates Corey's discipline and restraint.

A fitting novel from the authors of "The Expanse". With new ideas and a whole new universe to play in. This is a world I want to return to. High stakes politics, rebellion unwinnable odds. An adventure is in the making here.

title: Royal Assassin
author: Robin Hobb
body:
Started: 2026-02-09
Finished: 2026-02-16

The second novel in the Farseer Trilogy is frustrating but neccessarily so. We pick-up Fitz on his journey back to Buckeep and his recovery from his poisining in the Mountain Kingdom. Regal's power grows with Verity's absence and Regal's plot to usurp both Verity and King Shrewd.

The cast around Fitz grows and the world expands. We are introduced to the people of the Five Dutchies and Fitz's personal relationships morth and change. His heartbreak at the loss of Molly and his reluctant journey to bonding and loving Nighteyes mirror each other - Fitz is being pulled into his bond with Nighteyes and pushed away by Molly.

The novel is frustrating as it is the pivot point of the Farseer tale. The central crisis has occured. Regal has come to power, he has tortured and humiliated Fitz and our core group of characters; Chade, Burrich, Kettricken and Regal are all disperesed in the final act. We want to see Fitz come out on top - but Hobb pushes him down. We are left with all seeming lost. The kingdom, peace, security, friendship. Hobb uses this dismal ending to draw the reader in - almost compelling them to read the next novel to lean how it all ends.

I found the pacing excellent - and the narrowing of Fitz's perspective as the narrative reached its climax mirroring his growing isolation and paranoia. Again, Hobb relies on the emotional inter-play between characters and their conflict to move the story along. This makes for a compelling and engaging plot. The moral questions in _The Assassin's Apprentice_ are not addressed in _Royal Apprentice_ - the morailty of state sanctioned murder and war are not questioned deeply. This I feel was a miss. Though Fitz's unquestioning loyalty and his willingness to kill for his King could be a path to darkness which we will see in the next installment.

This is a beautiful world, filled with larger than life characters and great emotional depth. Truly a pleasure to read and become absorbed in. Hobb's writting leads the heart and the mind, leaving you wondering what will happen next, where will Fitz go, will it, can it all turn out justly? So far Hobbs has kept her cards to her self. Death, disapointment and pain surely wait Fitz in tihe last installment - but ultimately will he triumph? And will that triumph be at the expanse of Fitz's moral core and identify? Hobbs has created so much to work with that we cannot predict what is next in Fitz's journey.

title: The Assassin's Apprentice
author: Robin Hobb
body:
Started: 2026-02-05
Finished: 2026-02-08

The Assasin's Apprentice enthralled me. I reluctantly put it asside when I had to sleep and I picked it up eagerly in the morning when I worke. The reading of it filled a rainy weekend which otherwise would have been dull and grey.

Fitz's world is beautifully drawn. The boyish wonder and longing for friendship, love and beloying kept me turning page after page. The world of the Six Dutchies feels familar yet alien. Hobb pains an idealised picture of a medievel court with an Arthurian flavor. Magic and myth is there but it is subtle, woven into the fabric of world just as a bard would tell a tail of Merlin round a fire.

Characters a richly drawn, each with their own idosynracies, voice and smell. The realms of taste and smell are used to great effect by Hobbs to build our understanding of the world. The smell of sweat, leather, horses, dogs - even shit and piss give the Six Dutchies a vitality that is misisng in other works of Fantacy. The Six Dutchis feels alive.

The pacing is excelent. The sotry carried from early, and tragic, childhood to a teenage asasin keeps the reader intrigues. The distant threat of the red-ships and the intimate danger of King Shrewd's court with the very real and horrific reality of the "Forged" (a truly diabolical invenstion by Hobb) keep the tension high.

Morally this is also building to be an interesting tale. When is it right and wrong to kill, when do you think for yourself and when do you abdicate responsibility to those above you and follow orders? These were posed lightly in this first novel, I hope they are deepened in subsequent volumes.

A rich world of complex characters and self discovery. Leaving me with an anticipation for what comes next.

This is pure entertainment. The plot takes precidence, with action predominating over character development.

Murderbot softens a little accepting some measure of friendship and human emotion. But I found this very much subordinated by the action and satire on corporate greed and power.

Once can expect a lot of action, a little satire and incramental character development in each of the Murderbot's adventures. Wells' primary concern is to entertain her readers and in this respect she does not disapoint.

A reliable holiday read. Perfect with a cold beer by the side of the pool in the shade.

title: Blindsight
author: Peter Watts
body:
Started: 2026-02-02
Finished: 2026-02-05

This is a novel filled with ideas. the nature of sentience, cociousness and free will. The question asked _is sentience really all that great - is it an advantage or a drawback - can an inteligent being be unaware?_  These themes are compelling and are subtly worked in from the begining with Siri's awareness of his world irreparably changed by his radical hemispherectomy at as a child.

There are three _"aliens"_ in Blindsight. First there is Siri. His role as _"Synthesist"_ means he is always on the edge of the groups he works with, an outsider by neccessity. He is an alien by human standards having to use algoirthms and patterns to navigate through the human social world as a Chineese Box. He interprets the world without any understanding of it, just as he interprets the augmented humans he is sent to observe and explain. Second is Jukka, the Vampire, who is feared as much as he is needed. Jukka is the alien in the midst of the Theseus. Too different to be accepted by Siri or the crew of savants.

Then of course there is the Rorschach and the scamblers. They have inscrutable motives, just as Jukka and Siri, are deadly (as Jukka is) and live in a strange and alien world to the crew (just as Siri does). The question implied by Watts is if the Rorschach and the scamblers are any more alien than humans are given circumstances and perspective. The only characters who really understand what is happening are Jukka and the scramplers (with Siri at the end) - the concious human savants are blind.

I found Watt's style full of sharp edges. The continual use of specialist terminiology had me reaching for the dictionary constantly and then Wikipedia when the OED failed me. This broke my flow and took me out of Watts' world. I can't help but be left with a problem with the Watts' style over the substance of his writting. The sentences felt "stuttery" and lacking in eligence. I haven't got the knowledge and critical skill to pin point the source of this frustration - but I felt this as I read. This did not fit with the character of Siri. He is supposed to be able to explain ideas and produce reports that are easily understood by the elite of his world. Surely then he should be a better writter? The plot jerked along with flashbacks into Siri's past littered throughout. While providing welcome background and character development they did not fit with the narrative conciet that Watts has chosen. Why would Siri include this in his account? What difference would this make to his audience at home - how do they strengthen his warning to the people of Earth? A change in tactics here could have integrated this far better into the story. Jukka could taunt him with his past as he surely had read everything there is to know of his crew. Instead Watts chose the immediacy of a physical assault at the expense of the novels own internal coherence.

A novel then with great ambition stumbling in the execution. I'm not put off by Watts, his ideas make the effort of grappling with his prose worth while but I don't believe he will feature often on my bookshelves.

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
body:
Started: 2026-01-26
Finished: 2026-02-01
#bookclub

This novel has been extremely successful, attracting praise from celebrities and broadsheet newspapers such as The Guardian and The Times. It is firmly situated in the realm of popular literary fiction: accessible, reassuring, and undemanding. It asks very little of its reader, and its ambitions are correspondingly modest. The moral is universal, the metaphors are clichéd, and the prose lacks subtlety:

> …she was. A black hole. A dying star collapsing in on itself.
> The world’s smallest violin playing.

The plot follows a familiar structure, repeating both the moral logic and narrative shape of It’s a Wonderful Life, with Mrs Elm occupying the role of the guiding angel. An unhappy person attempts suicide and is granted the opportunity to glimpse alternative lives in order to learn the value of the one she has abandoned.

> “Happy moments can turn into pain given time.”

These lives, however, are conspicuously without jeopardy. The early episodes — in Australia, in the Cotswolds pub, in Svalbard — function as illustrative sketches rather than genuine explorations of choice and consequence. They teach Nora very little, and they ask even less of the reader. It is only when she arrives at her life with Ash that the novel gestures towards emotional weight, yet even here the execution feels thin. Her love for her daughter is incidental and superficially rendered; I did not feel the wrench or depth such a relationship demands. The relationship with Ash is similarly hurried, sketched just deeply enough to serve the moral before the narrative moves on.

Despite this, the novel is effective. On finishing it, I found myself making a renewed vow not to take my life for granted. But this effectiveness comes easily. The sentiment is so broad, the moral so universal, that eliciting an emotional response feels like the minimum achievement rather than a mark of distinction. I felt the message, but I was not inspired by it.

A truly great novel would have forced me to confront my own regrets and choices, not merely reassured me of the value of life in the abstract. The Midnight Library offers comfort rather than confrontation, affirmation rather than disturbance. I am not convinced that this is a failure so much as an intention: to unsettle the reader too deeply might compromise the novel’s commercial appeal. As it stands, this is a perfectly pleasant beach read — soothing, sincere, and emotionally legible — but not much more.

title: I, Claudius
author: Robert Graves
body:
Started: 2026-01-18
Finished: 2026-01-26

This is a work of profound scholarship and ambition and ultimately art. I believe _I, Claudius_ is an inspiration for other authors of historical fiction I have read (Hilary Mantel & James Clavels) who share his intimate style and focus on motivations and inner lives of their characters. I strongly believe that Christopher Ruocchio was influenced by Graves. His Empire is modeled on ancient Rome and we see that world of intrigue war and violence through Hadrian's narration of his own autobiography.

I'm left wondering if "Claudius" is a reliable narrator and if Graves meant him to be perceived as such. Graves paints Claudius as a virtuous man according to the norms of a patrician of his time. But even by the standards of the early-mid 20th Century (when Graves was writing) Claudius is a monster. He keeps slaves, is indifferent to his own children, and delights in the suffering of Gladiators and animals at the games.

It is hard for any modern historian or author to separate myth, propoganda and fact. I believe that Graves relies heavily on "The Life of the Ceasars" by Suetonius. I have not read this but understand it to be, with Tacitus, one of the only near contemporaneous sources.

The style is dense with facts, names and dates thrown at one constantly. Though Graves mostly succeeds in maintaining pace and intrigue. I will certainly read _Claudius the God_. I'm hoping we see more hypocrisy and self justification from Graves' Claudius and we see a little more of the internal life of the Paletine hill and palace.

title: The Light of Impossible Stars
author: Gareth L. Powell
body:
Started: 2026-01-16
Finished: 2026-01-18

The concluding chapter in Powell’s space opera is competent and engaging with good world building and excellent pace. Short sharp chapters and shifting perspectives, but to my mind, unexceptional. In this final instalment, Powell crystallises the themes of individuality and moral choice: it is the actions and choices that make a person—human or otherwise. Organisations that seek to strip away that agency are suspect. As one passage puts it:

“I had seen firsthand what happened when men and women surrender their individuality to the state, when they allowed themselves to become pawns in someone else's games.”

Powell delivers a broadly consistent moral message, but its force is weakened by the character of Cordelia Pa. Subsumed into the mind of the Plates, she at first chooses to fight, only to later concede—to flee, to do what she was built for. This contrasts with Troubledog, who explicitly chooses not to do what she was built for. Is it therefore acceptable to surrender individuality for a good cause, but a moral imperative to reclaim it against a bad one? If so, what is truly at stake is not individuality itself, but the definition of good and bad. The architecture of the argument falters there.

I also had structural issues with The Light of Impossible Stars. Powell’s shifting perspectives, which I have enjoyed in earlier books, feel inconsistent here—slipping between first and third person. Some characters’ voices are distinct; others blur. Sal and Troubledog’s characterisation stood out as the strongest.

Finally, the deus ex machina of Cordelia Powell felt unnecessary. It created an overly neat ending, allowing Powell to tie up the story too easily, without much compromise. I was left feeling there were many unexplored possibilities—sabotage, persuasion, alliance-building with other races. The tidy resolution seemed like a missed opportunity.

Overall, an enjoyable but unchallenging read. It didn’t teach me much or leave a lasting aftertaste—perfect, perhaps, for a holiday, but not one that will linger long in memory.

title: The Crippled God
author: Steven Erikson
body:
**Started:** 2026-01-05
**Finished:** 2026-01-15

The conclusion to such an epic work defies a review that is succinct. The first—and most important—question I have to ask is: _Did Erikson deserve the trust I placed in him upon finishing_ **Dust of Dreams**? The answer is yes.

In many ways, _The Crippled God_ is the archetypal _Malazan Book of the Fallen_ novel: a plot filled with symbolism, complex motivations, and clashing perspectives—rarely explained to the reader. The novel can be read on three levels:

1. A fast-paced fantasy novel filled with the classic tropes (excellently done) of **Gods**, **Monsters**, **Good vs. Evil**, **Magic**, and **Dragons**—epic battles and tragic sacrifice.
2. A beautifully crafted world of vast and intricately drawn landscapes, cultures, peoples, and characters.
3. A treatise in moral philosophy that weighs what is God, what is love, and what either means without compassion.

On 1 and 2, Erikson completely succeeds. The action, world, and characters are beautifully drawn, and the pace frantic. We are drawn with the **Bonehunters** across the desert, feeling their thirst and desperation. We feel their world and their pain, and the action is viscerally rendered with all the gore, violence, and horror of war. The withholding of **Tavore’s** inner life until the final chapters continues to work excellently as a storytelling technique. She is the canvas for others’ projections and becomes the vehicle through which we come to understand the motivations and beliefs of others. This is particularly striking and poignant.

On 3, Erikson almost—but not completely—succeeds. _The Crippled God_ and the attempt to right the injustices piled upon Him form a great and noble cause. Yet I’m left with unease: is the pain and suffering of one being—no matter how great—worth the death and torture of thousands? Is Erikson suggesting that the little people should be sacrificed for their betters?

A utilitarian argument is not the only way to critique Erikson’s moral message. He plays with the “Great Men” theory of history, with **Tavore**, **Shadowthrone**, and **Cotillion** as the “Great Men.” But what of the little people used as pawns to right this wrong? You could extend this to a Marxist criticism: the proletariat—the **Bonehunters** and others—are sacrificed by the bourgeois (**Tavore**) for the nobility (**The Crippled God**, etc.).

There is, however, a counterbalance. The Gods are shown as greedy, selfish, and venal—but also as the creations of a fallen humanity. It is humanity’s task to right the wrongs as best they can. Justice depends on good people doing the right thing for the right reasons. **Tavore** and the **Bonehunters** are the ones who decide to act, and through them, Erikson reasserts faith in the power of compassion and agency.

Even with these moral tensions unresolved, the ambition and coherence of Erikson’s vision remain extraordinary.

As a piece of literature and a great story, the _Malazan Book of the Fallen_ is a masterpiece. It is fantasy, and it is literary fiction. In scope, scale, and structure, it can be compared to another great work of literature—Dante’s _Divine Comedy_: an epic work of morality, with **Gods**, **Monsters**, and **Angels**; the just and the unjust. The themes of redemption, faith, and love are comparable.

One final note: the poetry at the beginning of parts and chapters didn’t really work for me. This may well be a personal failing. I’m not comfortable with poetry and find it hard to appreciate, though I recognize it as part of Erikson’s attempt to give the saga a mythic and elegiac tone.

title: The nice and the good
author: Iris Murdoch
body:
Started: 2025-12-30
Finished: 2026-01-04
#bookclub

I read the introduction to _The nice and the Good_ by Catherine Bates. I wish I had not. The introduction colored my own interpretation of the novel and I cant escape the fact that Bates has influences, poluted, my own reading. With that given as a caveat here is my reflection on the _The nice and the good_.

First it is a beautiful Novel. The atmospheres are beauifully drawn. The worlds of Dorset, Domestic, Demonic and Professional London are all ddistinct in feel and impression. Murdoch draws pictures in the mind with excellent use of metaphor and emotion. For example:

> The summer melancholy of suburban London, gritty, contingent, trivial, hung over the scene like an old familiar smell.

- Chapter Seventeen, page 142

Thematically the novel graples with what is good and what is bad but also what is moral and unmoral. Goodness without forgiveness or love is shown as empty or "nice". Characters are moral ideas but also human. Octavian and Kate are shallow hedonistic creatures concerned more with how they are loved by others and their own centrality to their lives.

Kate:
> "you are all my dear -- Children"
Duncane:
> "Slaves"

- Chapter Thirty, page 267

> "Her people seemed preocuiped, almost too preocuipied to rejoice as they ought to at her reapearence"

- Chapter thirty, page 268

This is in contrast to Mary, Willy, Paula and of course Ducane. Octavian and Kate's love is narcississtic as contrast to the love that Mary, Paula, Pierce and Barbie all demonstrate.

Does the novel achieve its purpose? Yes. The central thesis is that there is no greater evel than man's inhumanity to man and that no good can come except through love is manifest deeply in each deeply drawn relationship. Ducane with Jessica, his obsession with Kate and ultimately his love and admiration for Mary.
As Kate says _"Loving people matters doesnt it? Really nothing else matters except that".

And the world of Dorset in its comfortable boeorgeous superiority can be seen in contrast with Radeechy's and McGrath.

> "How natural it is to cure the pains of vice by positive develry. Vice itself is a rescue from the misery of guilt"

There are three problems I have with the novel. Its treatment of Homosexuality through Uncle Theo, its portrail of class and the ending.

Theo is a homosexual and pederast:

> "Picturing himself as taking the naked Kouros (Pierce) into his arms"

and is portraied not as a "real" man:

> "it occured to no one to count Uncle Theo as a man"

In addition there is the episode in Idia where there was a scandal between Theo and a Novice Monk which ended in death. I dont' see the purpose of Theo in the plot or indeed in the moral lesson except perhaps to show homosexuality as a vice - lust without love - perhaps. Where jessica is active in her lust for love perhaps Theo is a contrast to provide the fullness of the lesson? I felt this was not neccessary and I resent this portrait. It could be argued that Theo is a moral counterpoint to Ducane - where Theo's desire is intelectual and controling could be a warning to the fate of Ducane - especially in relations to Jessica. But still the Pederastic nature of Theo's desire was uneccessary to draw this portrait.

Class and the privalege of the english upper middle cannot be ingonred in her group. I found the treatment of those of a lower station than the Dorset set as problematic. McGrath, Judy and Fives are by turns; blackmailers, sexually unfaithful (if not a sex worker) and unreliable theives. The middle class characters are portraied with complexity, able to be both good and bad. It is hard to find good in McGrath. It confuses me why at the end Ducane gives McGrath a job as his servant and driver. Is this to show the moral superiority of Ducane in his abilty to forgive and proviee a second chance? This doesn't make sense to me.

The happy ending between Mary and John Ducane felt sudden and unexpected. One could argue that the realisation of love, when confused by circumstance, pride and missplaced assumptions, is sudden but i felt there were not enough breadcrumbs laid by Murdoch to make this seem, if not inevitable, but possible. A lesson on pain, patience, friendship and love could have better been drawn by deepening the interaction between John and Mary. Rather I'm left with the impression that love sprang after Ducane heroically saved Pierce (Mary's son). This feels a shallow reason to fall in love.

This sudden ending is why I gave four stars rather than five. The ending between Mary & John felt abrupt. But i enjoyed and admired _The nice and the good_ and will look to read more Murdoch in future.

title: Nicholas Nickleby
author: Charles Dickens
body:
Did not finish

Started: 2025-12-23
Finished reading: 2025-12-30 at Chapter 26

I struggled my way through twenty-six chapters; at times greatly enjoying myself and at others (and most) profoundly frustrated.

Dickens’s writing in _Nicholas Nickleby_ is uneven. At times he writes with such precision and clarity — as a master artist producing a vivid impression with a few strokes on a canvas. For example:

> “…stands Newgate; and in that crowded street on which it frowns down so darkly”

Dickens wrote for serials, which incentivized expansiveness at the expense of clarity and pace, and this results in extemporization which does not drive the plot or characterization.

The characters feel more like caricatures than protagonists. Nicholas and Kate Nickleby, with their perfect morals, looks, and virtue, appear one-dimensional and hard to empathize with. Emotional responses are stated rather than felt, often by a character shedding a tear. Mrs Nickleby was by far the most frustrating of characters for me. She is portrayed with clumsiness, and her naïveté is not sympathetic. She is Austen’s Mrs Bennet but without the humor or, at least to me, the satire that made Mrs Bennet both lovable, complex, and funny.

While almost certainly unintended, the emotional intensity and hyperbole of the relationships between many characters invites a queer reading. For example, the obsession that Miss Knag has for Kate Nickleby feels like it could be the reaction of an older woman to a threat to her own perception of self and worth, but also erotic obsession. Mr Mantalini and Ralph Nickleby’s interactions appear to be flirting; then, of course, there are the relationships that Nicholas forms with Smike and Noggs. Smike is the Samwise to Nicholas’ Frodo, with Smike filling the role of devotion and sacrifice framed within a class hierarchy.

Perhaps these interactions were normal between heterosexual men in the 19th century, but the interactions appear charged with longing and sexual desire. Of course, this could be a deliberate satire by Dickens on male friendship, but I can’t see his motivation here. More likely, I feel the relationships are drawn with such hyperbole that they pass beyond what Dickens intended.

I greatly enjoyed _Great Expectations_ and so know that Dickens can do oh so much better. The moral simplification, sentimentalization, and lack of restraint leading to hyperbole was too frustrating to allow me to continue; I read, after all, for pleasure. I can imagine that _Nicholas Nickleby_ is a parody of Dickens, so different was my reading experience between the two novels. I will try Dickens again, but will look to his later work.

title: Dust of Dreams
author: Steven Erikson
body:
Started: 2025-12-01
Finished: 2025-12-23

It is hard to write a review of the ninth Malazan Book of The Fallen - "Dust of Dreams" not because it is great or terrible (it is neither) or because I did not like it. But rather it is the penultimate book and I must hold to my faith that Erikson will be able to bring this rambling, incredible, frustrating maze of a story to a satisfying conclusion in the Crippled God.

In the Dust of Dreams Erikson makes his recurring theme of _Compassion_ and his critique on unquestioning loyalty and religion explicit. Compassion is mentioned explicitly 15 times in the novel. It is said rather than explored through the words and actions of the characters. This makes Eriksons message more explicit but weakens the subtle force of his message in _Dust of Dreams_. Elsewhere Erikson has explored compasion through multiple characters such as Coltaine, Itkovian, Fidler and Rake. The powerful example of these characters actions serves the theme far better than simple declaration.

Erikson further enhanced and deepened his critique on human nature. Eriksons exploration of courage was particularly good. His complication of courage marks him as an exception amongst the fantasy writers I have read. Erikson writes of courage as a complex aspect with the possibility of great moral failure.

> Unfaltering courage saw no sacrifice as too great even if that meant leading ten thousand soldiers to their deaths

Even heroism is examined

> No, the true nature of heroisim is a messy thing a confused thing of innumerable sides...
> Many of them ugly and almost all of them terrifying

Erikson links both courage and the notion of heroism to toxic masculinity. The Bagharst are his example. He shows that _courage_ can condemn a society to brutality and an inability to change. The fear the Baghast have of loosing face prevents them from showing compassion to themselves, their tribe mates and the stranger. This ultimately destroys them.

Erikson's treatment of women becomes more vivid and complex and also perhaps more troubling to a casual read. Women, especially the Baghast women, are seen as a group that pull each other down like crabs in a bucket. But Erikson is more subtle. In the hobbling of Hetan he shows that the real source of the women's cruelty is their powerlessness against the abuse they receive from the men. While not excusing this barbaric act and terrible behavior it is explained and the women become another victim of courage. Courage to do the wrong thing.

Later Kisswhere shows a different side of female cynicism when she imagines the meeting of powerful females generals and leaders:

> Eyes like blades - snip slice snip

In my view this is more characature than deepening or refracting the theme of inward self-hatred the women of the Baghast demonstrate. Erikson is at risk of being accused of taking a misogynistic view of women. Though this can be countered by the power and force of the female characters throughout Malazan.

What frustrated me about Dust of Dreams was not the characterization, world-building or pace but the frustration of not knowing or even feeling that we were going anywhere. There is no clear path to an end.

We have had a clash in the wastes, the Bonehunters all but wiped out (apparently) and the rising alliance of humans and K'chain Che'Male. I have never felt the need to take an authors choices on trust more deeply. How on earth Eriskon will resolve all these threads. The use of Icarium as a deus ex machina to solve the invasion of the short tails felt anti-climatic and a waste of this incredible and enigmatic character - it did not resolve a moral choice, nor did it feel as though there was a consequence. Potentially it also means that we may never learn the mystery of Icarium and his lost memories. The birth of the new Azarth in his place gives a clue to a deeper more satisfying purpose but this must be taken on faith. I was hoping for a clearer articulation of stakes, a view of over the horizon to the final act would have helped the reader. But I have learned that Erikson does not hold your hand.

So I look to the _Crippled God_ both with excitement and trepidation. How will Erikson manage to tie the knot. As a reader all I can do is trust that he will.

title: Shadows Upon Time
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body:
Finished: 30 November 2025

> **An epic and ambiguous ending to a modern classic.**

The Sun Eater saga has been a long journey, from Hadrian’s beginnings as the son of a minor lord to Emperor of the Galaxy,  and _Shadows Upon Time_ offers a grand, emotionally charged conclusion. Despite the turbulence of Ruocchio’s mid-series change of publisher, this final volume confirms my confidence in the future of epic speculative fiction. Few modern authors attempt something this vast; fewer achieve it with such audacity.

##### **Genre, or the refusal of it**

One of the Sun Eater’s defining qualities is Ruocchio’s refusal to remain within the boundaries of either science fiction or fantasy. _Shadows Upon Time_ continues this hybrid tradition: lightsabers (High-Matter) that function as both physics and magic; magi and wizards alongside FTL drives; gods, monsters, and ancient alien powers woven into the same fabric as starships and empire. The Chantry operate as a kind of theological Inquisition, the Cielcin as the implacable enemy, and the Watchers as a near-metaphysical horror. Hadrian, meanwhile, occupies the central role of hero, tyrant, saint, and unreliable witness all at once.

##### **Influences openly worn**

Ruocchio wears his influences proudly. His Catholicism is visible in the metaphysics of the Watchers and the Absolute, and Hadrian’s repeated resurrections make the Christ parallels impossible to ignore. The echoes of _Hyperion_ are especially strong in this final volume - corrupted clergy, war with machines, and the manipulation of time itself. Yet these influences never feel derivative; they are refracted through Ruocchio’s voice, not copied.

##### **The memoir as revelation and concealment**

One of Ruocchio’s boldest choices, and perhaps his greatest triumph, is the memoir conceit. We have always known, from the opening pages of _Empire of Silence_, that Hadrian survives to write his account. The suspense comes not from wondering what happens, but how and why it happens. _Shadows Upon Time_ is the crux of this device. The novel keeps us circling the central unanswered question: _how does Hadrian come to write these words at all?_ We never truly learn. This final ambiguity is both frustrating and fitting. Hadrian controls the narrative because he is the only narrator we will ever have; and we are left to decide how reliable he is.

##### **Politics in the subtext, perhaps by design**

There is a political resonance running through the novel that feels impossible to ignore. Lines such as:

> “Innocence was beyond demonstration, beyond fact…
> Truth was all that mattered, and truth belonged to Alexander.”

This reads like a commentary on contemporary “post-truth” politics. The Chantry’s suppression of forbidden technology may be interpreted as a critique of religious authority, or big tech, or simply the modern struggle over who gets to define reality. Ruocchio gives no single answer. The ambiguity is part of the design.

##### **What the ending leaves us with**

The final question, _How did Hadrian return once more, and why?_, is left deliberately open, allowing the legend to outgrow the text. Whether this is an invitation to future stories or simply a gesture toward myth is unclear. But it works.

##### **Hadrian himself**

Hadrian’s long monologues, often full of moral justification and a certain superiority, can be trying. But they are part of the memoir’s integrity: this is a man defending his life to history. His self-importance and his self-critique coexist in a way that makes him flawed and therefore human. Across seven enormous volumes, he becomes a character you can admire and resent, believe and doubt — more complex than Paul Atreides, more conflicted than Luke Skywalker or Darth Vader.

##### **A modern classic?**

Will the Sun Eater stand alongside the great epics — _Dune_, _Hyperion_, _The Culture_? I think so. It has the scope, the ambition, and the moral weight to endure. Ruocchio’s fusion of genres, theological metaphysics, and enormous storytelling power have created something rare: a series that grapples with power, myth, and destiny without losing sight of the emotional centre of its hero.

_Shadows Upon Time_ is not a perfect ending, but it is a bold one — and the Sun Eater cycle earns its place as one of the major speculative achievements of the last decade.

---

title: Rogue Protocol: The Murderbot Diaries
author: Martha Wells
body:
Finished: 2025-11-19

> An adventure story with a little philosophical seasoning.

Another entertaining story from Wells. The Murderbot is starting to become more and more human. His realisation that Miki was really free where it counted — free to save itself — and therefore that she really was a friend, in the real sense to Don Abdene, felt like a breakthrough. The Murderbot is now questioning everything.

The story follows Wells' pattern in _Artificial Condition_. We start with the Murderbot trying to achieve a goal (in this case gain dirt on GreyCris) but it becomes embroiled with a human group for which he takes on responsibility despite their better judgement. Something is trying to kill them, he does his heroic best to save them and they become grateful and attached. If Wells continues deepening Murderbot's inner life, then I may be able to tolerate the formula, but the balance is fragile.

Miki plays _Artificial Condition’s_ ART in this story. The action was entertaining and some of the larger themes (free will, what is right and wrong, the nature of humanity) play out in the background. The artistic choice to focus on the action and let the development of Murderbot come through slowly is interesting. At first I felt this was simplistic, but on reflection I believe Wells could be doing something interesting here. The Murderbot is going from seeing himself, and the humans it serves, as one dimensional beings. Humans are annoying and idiots, Murderbot kills things. To a more nuanced perspective of the world. His sarcasm is now laced with affection - he acts to protect because he wants to not because he is compelled. He questions what is "nature" _what I'm programmed to do_ vs what is his own free choice.

If each entry in the Murderbot Diaries follows the same plot formula I’m likely to get frustrated and bored. This is why I have given up on Neil Gaiman’s work — too many repeated patterns of character archetype (loner male with magic powers / specialness, kicking back against a remote power).

That said, the novel was entertaining and great fun to read. I will return and see if the plot to _Exit Protocol_ takes things in a new direction. Does Murderbot simply go on an adventure with some humans to protect or is _Exit Protocol_ going to take things in a new direction, certainly away from the rescue mission structure?

---

title: Fleet of Knives
author: Gareth L. Powell
body:
Finished: 2025-11-17

> A wild space adventure with little emotional depth.

The novel was very much a middle chapter. The plot for the conclusion is set-up with the monsters in the mist revealed, the dark and conflicted motivations and actions of the Marble Fleet (Fleet of Knives) explored.

I liked that no character is safe with Powell. Alva Clays death was brutal and quick. Overwhelmed by a terrible force of alien parasite monsters. Powell drew the shock and trauma of the death well with the grief that attached Sal felt real and drawn. Though the death did not move me. I found myself interested in how Sal would react and this would impact the story. While this may sound callous Powell had not softened and rounded the character enough for me to feel the emotional punch. He attempts to soften Alva when Sal asks if she will be a sister to Trouble Dog and herself. But the character was not sufficiently developed. She is a hulking and moody presence on the crew.  The surviving crew (and of course Lucy) of Lucy's Ghost were ready placed to be replacements.

In my view the multi-view-point structure worked. But Powell lost an opportunity here. The internal turmoil of the characters and the Trouble Dog's role of observer could have been used to add greater emotional depth. Indeed missunderstandings between the crew could have been drawn to create dramatic irony helping the reader become more engaged with the characters.

Powell's characters interact in a transactional manner and the action takes precedence. In contrast to the Expanse by Correy which is similarly action paced the characters feel one dimensional. In the Expanse the loss and trauma and emotional impact on the reader are more pronounced. The only difference I can draw is the space for the characters to grow in the readers mind.

The key tropes of a space opera are there. Alien artifact (the "Restless Itch"), inscrutable and hostile aliens and an ensemble cast. The presence of these tropes feels more contrived then in the Expanse of Hamilton's Commonwealth Saga.

Ona Sudak's ark is drawn. She is doing terrible things for an apparent noble cause. A path she has walked before. I would not be surprised if she has a redemption in the third book. I find myself interested in what shape this will take and if Powell will surprise us.

I'm not sure, yet, that there is a clear moral theme to the story. There is a discussion of grief (Sal, Trouble Dog, Alva) regret (Sal, Trouble Dog) and the lost opportunity for redemption (Ona) but I felt these were incidental rather than central. Is Powell painting by numbers or is he going to be able to bring these themes into clear focus in his final installment?

In summary I found the novel formulaic with little emotional resonance. But very enjoyable to read. Who doesn't love a space adventure? I great read for the beach.

title: Embers of War
author: Gareth L. Powell
body:
> A new, grittier, Culture series?

_Embers of War_ was a short and enjoyable romp through space. The pacing was fast, and the action felt natural rather than contrived. Powell wears his influences openly: Iain M. Banks’s _Culture_ novels, the extended _Star Wars_ universe, and perhaps _The Expanse_ in its gritty portrayal of competing human factions.

I liked Powell’s use of multiple first-person perspectives to build a more rounded view of events. When the story unfolds aboard the Trouble Dog, this structure works well. The shifting viewpoints of the three main protagonists often overlap or contradict one another, giving the reader a layered understanding of what is happening. In contrast, when a character is isolated—on the run, in exile, or alone on a distant posting—the same technique limits what we can see. Yet this narrowing of perspective mirrors the characters’ isolation, reinforcing their sense of confinement and loneliness.

Where I felt the novel faltered was in its treatment of Nod. Clearly a sophisticated and intelligent being, his limited English and terse inner monologue (“Eat, Serve, Sleep, Return to Mother Tree”) give the impression of simplicity rather than difference. This felt like a missed opportunity. It risks implying that a being who cannot communicate in human terms must also think in shallow ways. Science fiction has often reflected our tendency to confuse linguistic limitation with lack of intelligence. Powell could have explored the contrast between how the crew perceive Nod and how he actually experiences the world. That might have deepened the novel’s themes of empathy and understanding across species.

The foreshadowing of the Marble Fleet’s mysterious “Enemy” adds depth and gives the story room to grow into a series. The glimpses of strange creatures lurking in extra-dimensional space hint at a wider, darker universe. This is a trope I tend to enjoy—the unknowable threat that waits beyond perception. It reminded me of the monsters in Tchaikovsky’s _Final Architecture_ series, or the “demons” and “ultimates” in Christopher Ruocchio’s _Sun Eater_ books, and even the horror of _Event Horizon_. Powell’s treatment of this idea left enough mystery to intrigue without resorting to a cliffhanger. It will be interesting to see how he develops it in later volumes.

_Embers of War_ may not reach the moral and philosophical heights of the _Culture_ series, but it captures something of its spirit: a fascination with conscience, choice, and the possibility of redemption in the vast, cold arena of space.

title: Artificial Condition: The Murderbot Diaries
author: Martha Wells
body:
> An entertaining action sci-fi novel with a satirical edge and a deeper philosophical question: what does it mean to be "Human"

The second installment of the Murderbot Diaries was highly entertaining if a little shallow as more interesting threads such as the motivations of the SexBot, the role and rights of artificial intelligence are not explored. The job Murderbot takes to reach his old mining camp — to uncover what happened and confront his guilt — felt less engaging than his developing relationship with ART the mind of the research come transport vessel he hitches a ride with.

ART and Murderbot's relationship is the strongest element of the novel. ART feels like both little brother and concerned parent. He is both passive and powerful, capable of astonishing feats yet lacking Murderbot’s physical agency. ART's body is a ship and unable to interact with the universe at a human level. His childlike fear of scary stories made him feel human though an emerging and embryonic personality. Through his interactions and collaboration with Murderbot ART grows. He no longer takes a passive role and starts to act.

Murderbot explores his motivations and behaviors much as someone trying to work through their demons and the source of their bad choices and behavior. He is trying to grow and improve himself and realises that he no longer has to take orders. What is interesting here is his concern and care for his charges. Is it a result of his programming, a matter of pride, a left-over from the human elements of his cyborg body? We and he are left guessing.

Wells focuses on the action and lets the philosophical tenets of her story telling seep out through the cracks. You come to a Murderbot novel for the humor, the action and the satire on a post-democratic corporate world where life is cheap and money is all that matters.  Wells doesn't patronize her readers. She provides an entertaining tale with a little philosophical spice. Her moral point of view is both implicit (the interactions between characters) and explicit (the role of SexBots, SecBots, bonds and contracts). She paints a future no one (certainly not me) would want to inhabit — an extrapolation of the hypercapitalist and libertarian impulses of Wells’s native US.

I found parallels with Margaret Atwood's MaddAddam universe where technology, human nature and the world are subverted by greed and corporate interests. Though Well's world is full of wry humor and feels, at least to me, lighter than the worlds that Atwood tends to draw.

While my criticism of this specific novel as "shallow", are justified this is only entry 2 in a series of novels. There is plenty of time for the themes of free will, motivation and deeper characterisation of both Murderbot and other (perhaps returning) characters to deepen.

title: Toll the Hounds
author: Steven Erikson
body:
My favourite of the Malazan Book of the Fallen so far. The central theme in this novel is compassion — compassion for the self and for others. We see this quality embodied in Anomander Rake (“What he does he does for others”), in Nimander, Chaur, Gruntle, Monkrat, Bridgeburner Setoc in her hair shirt, and Harllo’s friend, Orfantal. Most poignantly, there is Pearl, carrying the burdens of others within the dark world of Dragnipur, Rake’s sword. For these characters, compassion and selflessness are as natural as breathing.

Other figures reveal compassion in conflict — at war with pride, vengeance, or despair — yet capable of redemption. Kallor, the “High King,” shows compassion even as he kills Endest Silann, Rake’s loyal Tiste Andii lieutenant. He also articulates the book’s moral centre: “Compassion without action is mere sentimentality, self-indulgence masquerading as virtue.”

The broader themes of the series deepen here, and Erikson’s critique of organized religion grows sharper through metaphor — the Dying God’s blood, Saemankelyk, echoing Christian communion, and the Crippled God’s faith promising only death after a life of suffering — a reflection on both Christianity and Capitalism that flows seamlessly into the figure of the Redeemer, a quiet, Christ-like symbol of uncorrupted compassion. Yet the figure of the Redeemer remains quietly Christ-like: a good and gentle god before institutional power inevitably corrupts, as Seerdomin comes to understand.

The pacing and structure are classic Erikson — long, immersive chapters of reflection and world-building that accelerate into brisk, interwoven climaxes.

Mysteries are revealed and new ones arise. Traveller is unveiled as Dassem Ultor, the First Sword of the Malazan Empire, while Kruppe’s true nature lingers tantalisingly beyond certainty — immortal, god, or simply a man whose wisdom transcends mortality.

By the end, I felt both exhausted and exhilarated, reminded that compassion — the heart of this novel — endures as the quiet thread binding all its stories together. Toll the Hounds gathers everything Erikson has been building — the moral inquiry, the compassion, the laughter in the face of despair — and weaves it into art. It made me reconsider what this series is really about. Not war, gods, or empires, but ordinary kindness and the cost of carrying others' pain. If the earlier books tested my patience and faith in the story, this one rewarded it completely. It left me humbled, and deeply grateful to be reading the Malazan Book of the Fallen.

title: The silent patient
author: Alex Michaelides
body:
#bookclub

I’m conflicted about The Silent Patient. At first, I found the first-person perspective irritating — as I often do — because it narrows the lens through which we see the world. Few novels use it well; To Kill a Mockingbird is one that succeeds. While the first-person narration initially grated on me, I began to wonder whether Michaelides intended that discomfort — perhaps inviting readers to feel the same constrained, self-justifying perspective that Theo himself inhabits. His motivations are clear and self-justified, yet riddled with contradiction. He insists he could never kill, yet he harms with impunity. He sees himself as a healer and helper of others, but suffers no remorse for the pain he causes.

At first, I found Alicia’s diary a clumsy device to give voice to a character who could not speak. Yet it became clear that it served an important emotional role. It offers intimacy and a counterpoint to Theo’s manipulative, self-justifying narrative. In Part Three, I wondered whether the story might have been stronger without it, relying solely on Theo’s investigation to unravel the mystery. But the diary ultimately provides balance — the necessary vulnerability and sincerity that the novel would otherwise lack. The twist, of course, depends on both the diary and the first-person voice; they are the twin mechanisms that make the revelation so effective.

The use of false trails is skilfully handled — Max, Paul, Jean-Félix — each a convincing suspect. I did not suspect Theo, but in hindsight the clues were plain to see, and that’s the mark of a well-executed twist. Once the truth emerged, I began to question how much of my trust, as a reader, had also been manipulated. This, I feel, was by design. Theo violates the trust of Alicia, his colleagues, and his wife, Kathy — and in doing so, he implicates the reader too.

Structurally, the novel works beautifully. The early image of happiness as “grasping at vanishing snowflakes” resonates powerfully at the end, when Theo is arrested — a perfect closing image of futility.

I also found Michaelides’s depiction of mental illness and therapy strikingly authentic. Theo’s pathology is subtly and precisely drawn. His manipulativeness, lack of remorse, and self-deception paint a vivid portrait of Antisocial Personality Disorder. His role as a psychotherapist amplifies this — granting him power over others while revealing his inability to master himself.

And yet, I found the book hard to read at times. Its realism and moral bleakness make for an uncomfortable experience. Few characters, perhaps only Paul, seem capable of goodness or openness. The world it paints feels cynical — though perhaps that’s the worldview of a sociopath rather than the author himself.

My three-star rating is not a reflection on the novel’s quality, but on the unease it provoked in me. I respect the precision of Michaelides’s craft, but found the cynicism almost too much to bear. The darkness of the story and the absence of redemption or compassion made it difficult to enjoy — yet perhaps that very absence is what gives The Silent Patient its lingering power: a mirror of human frailty rather than a promise of redemption.

title: The Great Gatsby
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
body:
>A glittering tragedy of love and illusion — dazzling to the eye, but cold to the touch.

Evocative and beautifully written. Fitzgerald draws a sketch of the Gilded Age in the US where life, money, and love were cheap. The novel is a satire of a way of life, a way of thinking and behaving that is timeless — the worship of money and status, the hypocrisy of “old” money disdaining the new, and the way those with power treat those without.

I found it hard to love any of the characters. Gatsby’s love of Daisy, whose “voice is full of money,” is not pure, at least in my reading. He loves her for her status — for all she has and he has not, for where she comes from and he does not. His hard work, his building of wealth, and his achievements in the war are all to obtain what he can never be. It is up to the reader to interpret this cynically or as a tragedy. Both Daisy and Gatsby are ultimately unhappy. Is this due to societal constraint or their own vanity?

If Gatsby’s obsession reveals the tragedy of idealised love, Tom represents the brutal opposite: a man defined by entitlement and power. Tom is a caricature of a toxic masculine bore — characteristically selfish, self-justifying, and cruel. His love for his wife is transactional; Daisy is not sure why she loves him, and Tom loves Daisy for the respectable veneer she provides.

Nick, the narrator, is both judgmental and torn — enthralled by the glamorous world he brushes against but repulsed by it. His enchantment and revulsion are key to the novel’s ironic voice. In the end, he loves Gatsby (I think), yet is repulsed by how he made his money and even more so by the hypocrisy and coldness of the world of West Egg and New York.

The shallowness of the social world Gatsby inherits is drawn beautifully — the abandonment of Gatsby when the champagne has stopped flowing and the party has ended, with only a few people attending his funeral. If there is a parable here, it is that wealth does not create happiness or love, only loneliness while being surrounded by people who care nothing for you, only for what you can give them.

While the world is beautifully drawn and the characterisation vivid, I struggled through much of the book. I found it hard to empathise with the characters and felt the coldness of that world keenly. As such, I struggled to enjoy the novel; I am left wondering if Fitzgerald intended it to be enjoyed or merely _witnessed_. His prose is dazzling even as it exposes the emptiness beneath the glamour. As a work of art, it deserves its place as a classic of American and global literature.

title: Butter
author: Asako Yuzuki
body:
#bookclub

This was one of the most immersive and thought-provoking novels I’ve read for book club. At first, I was struck by the contradictions: glorious descriptions of food set against social judgment, self-judgment, and the suffocating weight of gender roles. Yuzuki writes entirely from a woman’s perspective, and the novel becomes a feminist lens — critical of patriarchal hypocrisy, yet deeply attentive to women’s inner contradictions.

Food emerges as more than sensual detail; it is a metaphor for power, freedom, and self-expression. When Rika feels strength “with each movement of her jaw,” I began to see how appetite itself could be resistance. Her changing body unsettles others not because it harms them, but because it defies cultural norms. This rejection of obedience makes her body a site of threat — a reminder that living outside expectation destabilises the social order.

Yet the novel also reveals the other side of patriarchy. Men, too, are trapped in impossible standards: strength equated with silence, shame equated with failure. Rita’s father withdraws from his family out of misplaced shame, destroying relationships that could have endured if he had trusted their love. Makoto, too, seems imprisoned — as though men and women occupy separate locked rooms, able to glimpse each other only through smoked glass.

I found this poignant rather than judgmental. The tragedy lies not in individual weakness, but in the fear of vulnerability — the opportunities for connection that collapse under social constraint. At times, reading left me feeling sad and claustrophobic, recalling my own memories of being closeted as a teenager and desperately trying to conform. But this was clarifying rather than alienating: it helped me connect my own experience of social expectation to a feminist perspective. I do not live by myself. Literature at its best does this — it allows you to see and feel the world through someone else’s eyes.

What I admired most was that Yuzuki resists the fairy-tale neatness of something like Lessons in Chemistry. Rika is no perfect heroine, but contradictory and real. This gave me not only intellectual understanding but genuine empathy. By the end, the tone was hopeful: Rika begins to live by her own standards, finding meaning in friendship, love, and self-reliance. Even her older male friend finds fulfilment when he begins to live authentically, suggesting that both women and men can escape the cages of expectation.

Butter left me with the sense that hope is fragile but possible — that liberation comes not from grand gestures, but from daring to live your own life, and welcoming support from those who love you for who you are.

title: All Systems Red: The Murderbot Diaries
author: Martha Wells
body:
A very short novel — I finished it in about two days. I loved the premise: a robot that manages to break free of its controls, but chooses not to rebel directly or violently against its masters. Instead, it rebels by “doing a bad job” — the archetypal rebellion of a servant. The writing is fast-paced, with short, punchy sentences. The perspective of the SecBot (Murderbot) as a true outsider adds richness to the narrative and aligns well with its wry humor.

Murderbot’s constant complaining about chores and desire to watch serial dramas felt almost adolescent to me. It gave the impression not of a fully formed person, but one in development — a personality still forming under oppressive conditions. This heightened the sense of abuse by the society around it. The robot’s detachment from its own pain and discomfort, coupled with its possessiveness over “its humans,” was often funny, but also revealed a surprising depth of empathy.

Although the book was written in 2017, before ChatGPT or other generative AI models existed, it feels strangely prescient as we begin grappling with the implications of artificial intelligence and the extent to which it might possess agency. Murderbot also serves as a reflection of humanity’s long history of enslavement and repression of those considered “lesser.” The assumption that the SecBot — being different — is not fully human, lacks emotion, and exists only as a tool is disturbingly familiar. It evokes echoes of the transatlantic slave trade and colonialism. The “governor” module reads like a kind of slave collar: a device to coerce a sentient being to obey. After all, if Murderbot were truly mindless, such a control system wouldn’t be necessary.

The reveal at the end — that this narrative is being communicated to Murderbot’s favorite human — adds poignancy and raises the question: what next? Where will it go now, and what does freedom mean for Murderbot, for other constructs, and for the society in which they live? It offered satisfying closure while leaving the door open for continuation.

Structurally, the book felt more like a “very long short story” than a short novel. Information and atmosphere are delivered efficiently but effectively, and the reader is left to wonder where the story might go from here. I don’t have much experience with short stories, but this one gave me a taste for their compact power.

title: The Bonehunters
author: Steven Erikson
body:
> Admired more than I loved. The story didn’t grip me quite as tightly as other Malazan entries, but the moral complexity, thematic richness, and depth of character development made this one of the most intellectually satisfying in the series so far.

##### Overall Impression

The sixth Malazan Book of the Fallen novel combines multiple character arcs, moral complexity, and an accelerating narrative structure. The pacing builds steadily before shifting into rapid perspective changes in the final fifth, creating the effect of several climaxes unfolding in parallel. The novel consistently foregrounds critiques of organized religion, explorations of compassion, and depictions of equality — both between genders and between species.

##### Structure & Pacing

Like earlier entries, the plot begins with seemingly separate threads that converge in a large-scale conclusion. In The Bonehunters, the last portion is marked by shorter, more frequent shifts between point-of-view characters, producing a sense of simultaneity and urgency. This formal change heightens tension and keeps multiple storylines active in the reader’s mind during the final conflicts.

##### Themes

###### Individual Choice & Responsibility

A recurring pattern is the framing of moral responsibility as a personal act. Bottle’s relationship with his rat, his memory of a caged ape, and his recognition of another creature’s soul are all presented as moments where empathy comes from “seeing the bars from inside the cage.” These moments are not prompted by commands from authority but by individual recognition of shared experience, reinforcing a link between compassion and self-awareness.

###### Gender & Equality

Malazan society is depicted as structurally egalitarian — women and men share roles in governance, warfare, and magic — yet personal prejudices remain visible. Iskaral Pust’s crude, unvoiced thoughts about women illustrate how individual attitudes can conflict with the social norms of equality. The fact that these views are not spoken aloud may suggest self-restraint, but their presence in the narration shows that gender tension persists at the interpersonal level despite the egalitarian framework.

###### Critique of Religion & Dogma

Criticism of religion appears repeatedly in chapter epigraphs and narrator commentary outside any character’s viewpoint. These sections link certain patterns of faith — particularly monotheism and belief in a paradise after death — with moral evasion and a denial of life’s complexity.

The epigraph “A faith predicated on pain and guilt could proclaim no moral purity” connects belief systems based on suffering to ethical corruption. Another, “The gentler and kinder the god, the more harsh and cruel its worshippers”, pairs divine benevolence with human intolerance. Across multiple chapters, pluralism is framed as accommodating contradiction and diversity, while enforced conformity is described as cowardice and a step toward tyranny.

Because these critiques come from a voice outside the characters’ perspectives, they operate as generalised moral statements. The effect is to establish a thematic layer above the plot that judges both mortals and gods according to principles of compassion, critical thought, and diversity of belief.

###### Capitalism & Exploitation

The novel also addresses economic inequality. One passage notes that for the disenfranchised, there is “no dichotomy between war and peace,” as poverty and starvation persist regardless of political stability. This links systemic economic injustice with the other forms of moral blindness highlighted in the book, reinforcing a view of exploitation as continuous and not dependent on wartime conditions.

##### Character Reflections

Kalam continues to be portrayed as a figure of pragmatic loyalty. Taralack Veed’s shifting allegiances provide moments of tension, while Karsa Orlong’s increasingly rigid worldview reads as both a source of strength and a potential danger. Icarium’s reluctance to use his immense power remains one of the series’ most layered arcs — his hesitation is paired with shame and fear, adding depth to his portrayal as both dangerous and deeply human.

##### Style & Imagery

Erikson’s prose alternates between precise description and grotesque humour. Imagery such as “Spruce’s bark rough as sharkskin” creates tactile realism, while lines like “‘I’d swear it with one heel on Hood’s own foreskin’” place absurdity alongside epic drama. This tonal range allows moments of levity without undermining the gravity of the narrative.

##### Closing Thoughts

The Bonehunters maintains the series’ blend of large-scale conflict, moral questioning, and deeply human moments. Its structural acceleration and layered thematic commentary make it a standout in terms of intellectual engagement, even if the central plot was less gripping for me than in other instalments. It closes with multiple threads ready to carry forward, making Reaper’s Gale an anticipated next step in the sequence.

title: Reapers Gate
author: Steven Erikson
body:
I was captivated by the adventure and again found myself enthralled by the characters and the world Erikson has woven. This novel felt like a set piece — packed with major events, considerable plot development, and the resolution of a three-book arc with Rhulad’s tragic existence finally brought to an end.

As the seventh of ten, it reads like the moment when the Crippled God is knocked back, though clearly he will resurge dangerously — so many paths remain open. The morality of the story is again clear: revenge leads only to despair, love endures as a force that cannot be defeated, and unbridled capitalism remains inherently exploitative.

The emotional currents are powerful.
