The arc of the Sengar brothers is particularly poignant: Fear dies without fulfilling his quest, disillusioned with Seren Pedac as the focus of his purpose; Trull finds love but dies before he can build a new life; and Rhulad’s vanity and desperate need to be perceived as strong prevent him from breaking his cycle, making him the embodiment of brute power and thoughtless violence. Alongside these tragedies, the return of Hedge and his reunion with Fiddler, as well as the tragic yet redemptive journeys of Seren Pedac and Udinaas, provided moments of deep humanity.

Erikson manages the emotional pitch with care. The horror — such as the torture inflicted by the Patriotists — is juxtaposed with humour from Hedge, Fiddler, Tehol, and Bugg, creating tonal balance without diminishing either.

His narrative style keeps the reader guessing. Each new segment begins with a description of place, leaving us momentarily unmoored before characters emerge. This creates a slow reveal, a focusing-in, that mirrors the way we piece together his vast world.

Reaper’s Gale stands as one of Erikson’s most morally complex and emotionally resonant works so far.

title: Ready Player One
body:
The book’s three-part structure worked well, each section mirroring the quest stages within the OASIS and Wade’s experiences in the real world.

- **Part 1 builds the world**, context, and Wade/Parzival’s backstory. In both the OASIS and real life, this is about finding his footing — completing the first quest stage while establishing himself as a “gunter.”

- **Part 2 sees the pace pick up**. In the OASIS, Wade completes two more quest stages; in reality, he faces a direct confrontation with IOI and deepens his relationships with the other “Top 5.”

- **Part 3 mirrors a quest of three final stages**: his romance with Art3mis, the move to Columbus, and his infiltration of IOI as an indentured worker.

I was struck by the similarities with two 20th-century classics, suggesting Ready Player One may have been influenced by them:

1. 1984 — IOI’s power and surveillance echo the Party’s control, and the poverty and hopelessness of everyday life parallel that of the proles. Wade’s time as an indentured worker strongly recalls Winston Smith’s situation. The difference is in tone: Wade wins, where Winston is destroyed.

2. The Catcher in the Rye — Wade’s outsider perspective, social isolation, and confusion with the world mirror Holden’s alienation and critical eye.

The pacing was excellent, reading as much like a thriller as science fiction. The critique of late-stage capitalism was clear, especially in its portrayal of the exploitation of the indentured and the casual disregard for “ordinary” people.

##### Reflection

Ready Player One was far more than just a nostalgia-fuelled adventure. It blended the energy of a treasure hunt with sharp social commentary, weaving in themes of isolation, surveillance, and systemic injustice without losing its momentum. I came away impressed by how well it balanced excitement with depth.

title: A Desolation Called Peace
author: Arkady Martine
body:
This novel explores individuality and personhood with greater depth than its predecessor, especially through Mahit’s evolving relationships with both Yskandr and Three Seagrass. The Aliens (the Enemy) and Twenty Cicada (Swarm) bring these questions to a natural and compelling conclusion.

The love story between Mahit and Reed (Three Seagrass) felt organic and unforced. The sex scene — the first lesbian sex scene I’ve read — was visceral and real without being pornographic. It felt human. Martine’s female perspective is unmistakable throughout; she writes female characters with precision, empathy, and emotional depth.

Eight Antidote is particularly well drawn. His youthful sense of morality plays beautifully against the palace’s layered, often cynical politics. In contrast, I didn’t feel Martine’s male characters were as fully realised. They often seemed to occupy the background and lacked a kind of male energy — a presence of movement, speech, or desire — that’s difficult to pin down. Their motivations and internal conflicts felt more implied than explored. This may have been a deliberate choice.

The Teixcalaanli world itself leans into the poetic, refined, and graceful — qualities that feel feminine in tone — even as it's underpinned by a martial, imperialist structure. That tension is fascinating. Six Direction, the previous emperor, was an exception: complex and well developed. So too was the imperial associate. And One Lightning’s arrogance and brute force could be read as a deliberate critique of a certain vision of maleness.

This emphasis makes sense from an author writing from a feminist — possibly queer-feminist — point of view. The pacing was excellent. The shifts between The Jewel of the World, Lsel Station, and the fleet kept the tension high and the pages turning. A colourful book, rich in intrigue and adventure. Thoroughly enjoyed.

title: Midnight Tides
author: Steven Erikson
body:
I was initially disappointed by the absence of the Bridgeburners and other familiar faces — Erikson's abrupt shift to an entirely new continent and cast felt like a gamble. But by the end, I found myself deeply moved and impressed. Midnight Tides introduced a fresh, richly drawn group of characters, and among them, Trull Sengar stood out: a man caught between duty, justice, and conscience, whose internal struggle shaped much of the novel’s emotional heart.

The three Letherii brothers — Tehol, Hull, and Brys — were vivid contrasts. Hull, consumed by bitterness and revenge; Tehol, subversive and sardonic; and Brys, the King's Champion, whose quiet loyalty and moral clarity made him a surprising anchor to the story. Their differing paths created a layered portrait of what loyalty, resistance, and integrity can mean in a crumbling empire.

The narrative’s structure — told from multiple perspectives including Trull, Rhulad, Tehol (with Bugg), and Brys, along with key voices like Udinaas and Seren Pedac — was classic Erikson. Each arc ends on a cliffhanger, and each perspective deepens the thematic complexity. The first third of the book took time to build, but once the emotional connections settled in, the pacing became relentless. I was turning pages as much out of concern for the characters as for plot resolution.

The world of Lether and the Edur felt narrower and more insular than the grand scope of the first four Malazan books. But this felt intentional — a portrait of two civilizations locked into self-delusion and isolation, unable to see the wider consequences of their obsessions with commerce, conquest, and prophecy.

I was especially struck by Erikson’s critique of unbridled capitalism, rigid loyalty, and the hunger for power as an end in itself. Rhulad’s fate is one of the most tragic I’ve encountered in fantasy — a horror not of blood, but of being devoured from within by the very forces that promised salvation.

Midnight Tides may have begun as the outlier in the series, but by the end, it earned its place — strange, darkly comic, and emotionally searing.

title: House of chains
author: Steven Erikson
body:
Another enjoyable read from Erikson. Yet again I was impressed by the scope and scale of his tale. His building of atmosphere was very strong. Rakaru’s aura of menace and intense paranoia came across well.

The parallel and sometimes interwoven plots were enjoyable and the focus on the character development of characters good. The three transformations that Karsa went through: arrogant warrior, quiet guardian to rebel against a good were good narrative devices but I did t get enough sense of his inner life and conflict except that he did not want to be chained. Further we never got a sense (at least in my reading) of Tavore’s motivation. Why did she betray her family. Was it really just naked ambition.

The use of the dead of Rakaru as a deus ex machina was not a plot device I enjoyed. It felt to easy and contrived. The victory to easy. It did however mean that we didn’t get a real sense of Tavore’s worth as a commander. Though we have hints that she expected the Holy desert to support her.

The pacing was good. At over 1,000 pages this is a long book but at no point did I feel as though I would rather be doing something other than enjoying this story.

title: Fundamentally
author: Nussaibah Younis
body:
#bookclub

This short novel orbits a tight cast of characters, some of whom felt exaggerated to the point of caricature. Lionie (Nadia’s boss) and Tom (the macho security guy) especially seemed sketched to make a satirical point rather than emerge as fully formed individuals.

Much of the humor relies on cultural and tonal dissonance: Sara’s brash East London persona inserted into a Baghdad aid mission, and Nadia, a lapsed Muslim, mocking her heritage with secular detachment. I often laughed — but couldn’t always tell if I was laughing with these women or at them. Is the novel offering solidarity through irony, or critique masquerading as comedy?

The highlight for me was Nadia’s journey toward self-awareness. Her realization that she’s neglected others while clinging to Sara out of selfish need was honest and affecting — it gave emotional heft to a story that could otherwise veer into satire alone.

But I kept returning to the book’s portrayal of Iraq itself. The country and its people felt underdeveloped — more like a setting than a presence. Given Younis’ background as an aid worker in Iraq, I expected more complexity. Is the shallow depiction a reflection of Nadia’s limited worldview, blinkered by her UN bubble? Or is it a sign of the author’s own disengagement from the very world she’s writing about?

The sympathetic Iraqi driver/security guard, Farris, is drawn with warmth — but even he feels slightly idealized, more emblematic than individual. His presence hints at the richness of Iraqi life beyond the UN compound, yet he remains at the story’s margins. That left me wondering: is this narrative distance a deliberate reflection of Nadia’s limited viewpoint, or does it reveal a blind spot in the novel itself?

Fundamentally left me more uncertain than satisfied — but perhaps that’s part of the point. It opens up important questions about identity, satire, and complicity that linger well beyond the final page.

title: Memories of ice
author: Steven Erickson
body: I can see why people are so passionate about the Malian book of the fallen. The world building is exquisite. Each place felt real. I could smell the air feel the texture of the grass the feel of the city or field. The strength of humanity and our capacity for compassion and possibility of redemption were the key themes. The book is long - over 1000 pages yet I felt the pace was well done. The shifting perspectives and plot lines kept me intrigued and turning the next page.

title: Absolution Gap
author: Alastair Reynolds
body:
Reynolds closes his Revelation Space cycle with a novel that manages to feel both vast and oddly intimate. On the grand-opera side we get kilometre-high cathedrals trundling across a moon, spectacular lighthugger battles, and the slow, chilling reveal of just how hard the Inhibitors will fight. Yet the book’s heart is personal: the water-world interlude with the Jugglers and Clavain’s quiet sacrifice reads like a chamber piece, and the chapter-by-chapter date stamps hide a sly time-loop puzzle that rewards careful readers (I grinned when I realised Rashmika was Aura).

Character work is stronger than in the earlier volumes. “Pig” Scorpio’s arc—from hate-fuelled enforcer to someone willing to live for others—gives the series its most satisfying redemption. Captain Brannigan’s gradual merger with his lighthugger adds a delicious SF wrinkle: a crew literally becoming their ship. Even the Surgeon General, equal parts menace and comic relief, lands as an entertaining pantomime villain.

Is it great literature? Probably not. The prose can be functional and the plot occasionally overstuffed. But as immersive, idea-rich space opera it kept me racing through pages, delighted by the constant toggling between cosmic stakes and needle-point human dilemmas. I’ll happily sign up for another Reynolds voyage.

title: A memory called empire
author: Arkady Martine
body: Beautifully written and vivid. The world of Teixcalaanli was beautifully rendered and the characters well drawn. The plot was intense. A week of manic activity culminating with a sacrifice. The use of poetry was well done and made for an interesting way of making a culture feel both foreign and familiar. I don’t think Arkady fully explored themes of identity and belonging. The Imago machines gave a great opportunity to fully explore the sense of self and what I and we really mean. I feel this was a lost opportunity.

title: Redemption Ark
author: Alastair Reynolds
body: This was most certainly a middle novel. The plot was structured in the classic three part structure with the end and the climax thrilling. The nature of the treat to humanity and life in general set against the petty lives and concerns of politics were well drawn. Unfortunately I forgot to write my review of this novel directly after reading so my insights are somewhat diminished. However a thoroughly enjoyable read and I will be going back to finish the series. Hopefully my thoughts on that book will be more in depth.

title: Deadhouse Gates
author: Steven Erikson
body:
I wasn't expecting to enjoy this novel as much as I did. The booktube community told me that it takes at least until book 3 before you start to really "get" and love the "Malazan Book of the Fallen" series. I loved this novel. The sweeping scope, the morally complex and thoroughly human characters the capraciousness of the gods. I also enjoy the way that the magic system is as yet inexplicable. Its magic. It shouldn't make sense. I'm also looking forward to the fact that I will learn more.

The tragedy of the march of the dogs was poinent and the sacrafice of the Wickan Clans and the 7th were deeply moving. The cowardice and incompetence of the nepotistic nobles and High Fist was plane to see (as a Social Democrat and Republican in the British sense I related very well to that theme).

I felt the pacing was well done, at each point I wanted to know what happened next to each of the main bands of characters. Will the intersect, will they go off on their seperate paths. I was also happy that there were returning characters in this novel. I had been led to believe that this was a completely standalone novel. So it was great to see the return of Crokus, Aspalar (AKA Sorry), Fiddler and Kalam from the Bridge Burners. I found the relationship between Coltrain and Bulk his uncle amusing. Looking forward to the next installment.

title: Tai-Pan
author: James Clavell
body:
Enjoyed this a great deal. Again James a label brings the past to life. The swathing stinking world of the early 19th century on south China and Hong Kong. At first I struggled with the dialects that the characters speak but after 50 or so pages I found myself reading and understanding easily.

Yet again Clavell is fascinated by the differences in western and Asian thought and philosophy and again the appalling personal hygiene and stink of the Europeans comes up again and again.

title: Slaughter house 5
author: Kurt Vonnegut
body:
#bookclub

Enjoyed this a great deal. I enjoyed the ambiguity. Is the main character delusional or is he really jumping through time; has he really been abducted by aliens and put in a zoo or is this his psyche’s way of processing the trauma that he has been through in the war.

One of my reflections is that the structure of experiences reflects the situation in the war.

- He is conscripted - he is abducted by aliens

- He is has a forced march through enemy territory held by a group of soldiers who neither want him or care for him - he is carried in a spaceship over which he has no control of his journey. This is also reflected in his time jumps which one could also see as a form of dissociation.

- He is imprisoned in prisoner of war camps - he is held in a zoo by the aliens.

The novel is also a clear anti-war story. The depictions of the cruelty and futility of war are well made. The comparisons between conventional and nuclear weapons are very clearly made irrelevant. It’s not how death and suffering that is produced that is important but its scale. Any death and pain in war is too much, but the scale of Dresden and Nagasaki / Hiroshima are grotesque. The only difference between them is the method of that violation.

The visual imagery was also well done. I felt and smelt the world around me building vivid pictures in my mind. The voice of the narrator was clear. The voices of the other characters distinct.

Though this was a very short novel the scope was impressive. The scale of the plot and pace packed into a short 200 odd pages made the novel feel longer than it was. Not that it was too long, just I finished this novel feeling I had had a long journey that I was reluctant to finish.

title: Revelation space
author: Alastair Reynolds
body: A solid space opera that at times feels vast but zooms down into the psychology and motivations of a few key players. I felt the world could have been more fully drawn and the nature of the different political groups / human sects more fully. The pacing was good. It kept me turning the pages and I found it easy to imagine e the characters. Will return to read the rest of the series.

title: Gardens Of The Moon
author: Steven Erikson
body: Solid 3 stars. I thoroughly enjoyed the world-building, the characters, and the pacing. The story progressed swiftly, seamlessly transitioning between different groups and characters. I appreciated the way we were immediately thrust into the action. It’s similar to how history and life unfold—without the need for backstory or exposition. It’s as if we were watching Eastenders today. The large cast of characters felt well-developed and balanced. Some, by necessity, were straightforward and had unclear intentions. We don’t know their intentions as much as we don’t know the intentions of most people in our social circle. The only part where I felt a bit lost was towards the end when the Tyrant was killed. I missed the “house” that captured him amidst the rapid pace and action. I’m certain I’ll read more novels in the series, but I won’t be jumping straight back in.

title: The evolutionary void
author: Peter F Hamilton
body:
Another satifisfying read from Peter Hamilton. The pacing was excellent and the story structure of the novel kept me engaged throughout. I was excited to go back to the Commonwealth Universe every day. The characterisations especially of Animandia were particually strong and as ever the world building excellent. It was nice to see my old heros from the Pandora's Star and Judas Unchained back in action again. Paula is an excellent protagonist. I enjoyed the multi-threaded plot and again the Eduard intervals and stories.

The super high technology was interesting and much reminded me of "The Culture". It showed that no matter how wealthy or advanced a society it is always the struggle of ideas and of indiviual agency that are important. We can't out-evolve being human.

title: The Name of the Rose
author: Umberto Eco
body:
This was a very different book to the historical novels that I have read in the past.

##### What I liked

I loved the vivid and beautiful descriptions, the world of the Abbey would beautifuly realised, the atmosphere thick and vivid. The characterisation of Adso and Willimam beautifully drawn. The prejudicies and preocupations of people at the time was well explained and the distain that the learned and noble had for "The Simple" was cruel and simplistic, just as I had understood it to be in my limited knowledge of the middle ages. The mystery was allso well done. Even though Jorge was delightfully sinister I didn't suspect him, because of his age and blindness of causing the chatastrophic series of events.

##### What I didn't like

The quotations in Latin. As all the characters in the book would have spoken to each other in Latin, the quotations in Latin which were often not translated were frustrating. It felt like an act of intelectual snobbery by Eco. While it was often possible to infer the meaning of the quotes from context this was not always possible. While not understanding them did not mean that I could not follow the story I felt excluded from a complete understanding of the characters and their lives.

title: Patriot
author: Alexei Navalny
body:
#bookclub

This is only the fourth autobiography I have read (John Simpson, Malcolm X, John Waters & Alexei Navalny). I can’t say that I “enjoyed” the read given the terrible things that were done w to Navalny. But his story is inspiring. I got a glimpse of what integrity and real bravery feels like. I feel incredibly lucky to live in the UK. I’m reminded of two things. Obama’s quote that “the curve of history bends towards justice” or something similar. And the slogan from the Spanish civil war and countless others campaigning for freedom and justice “it is better to die on your feet than live on your knees”.

title: The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle
author: Haruki Murakam
body:
#bookclub

This book spancs many genres. Psychological thriller, fantasy, romance and science fiction and mystery. The weaves these styles together to build a character study of Toru Okada. Characters are told very much from Toru's perspective. Or in letters that he recieves from other characters. At many points in the story, especially in Book 2 and most of Book 3 I was convinced that Toru was insane, that he had suffered a psychotic break and his internal world was completely dissociated from the real world. The ending though shows that maybe this was just real.

The book leaves many questions unanswered. Does Noboru Wataya have super-human powers or is his personality and ruthlessness what breaks Kumiko Okada? Are Creta and Malta Kano really psychic or just confidence trixters, did Malta really enter Toru's dream and have sex with him?

I felt the treatments of Malta's defilement or rape by Nororu is interesting. The sense earlier on in the book was that this was all brushed over by Creta from commercial neccesity, a coldness towards her sister. When Malta realy needed Creta she was not there. I felt this may be another way she had betrayed her sister.

The role of May is interesting. The torture of Toru by leaving him down the well and the death of her boyfriend are suggestive as are her letters. Is she just a troubled teenager or is she something more sinister?

I enjoyed the book, never knowing which way the plot would go and what was real and what was not. In some ways it reminded me of Shutter Island by Dennis Lehane.

title: The Temporal Void
author: Peter F Hamilton
body: Another enjoyable read. The pacing was excellent. I again found the Eduard sections compelling. The mix of SciFi and fantasy worked really well. Especially as they are quarantined from each other. I’m glad the SI has reappeared. I wondered where they had gone. Very much a middle book.

title: The Stone Sky
author: N.K. Jemisin
body: This was a good end to the series. The confrontation between Essun and Nassun felt inevitable (from the first book really) but I found the redemptive ark of Schaffa fullfilling. I came to enjoy the first person narrative and the voice of Hoa. I found the exploration of the nature of freedom, free will and nature of life well drawn. I felt that the death of Jija did not have the emotional impact that I was expecting. I suspect this is because Jemisin did not draw out the inner conflict that must have been present in Jija enough, and given the perspective of Hoa as narrator this was probably not easy to do. Stone Sky felt like a fitting finale to a very enjoyable series. The centrality of female characters, the treastment of minorities and relationships, consent and love all beautifuly drawn. The narrative felt free of judgement. But the supporting cast of characters outside of Nassun and Essun did feel a little flat.

title: The Dreaming Void
author: Peter F Hamilton
body: Highly enjoyable space opera that cobines SciFi with Fantasy. The books are fast paced and highly exciting. I found myself looking forward to the Edeard sections a great deal. The similarities with humanity in this later Commonwealth are similar to the "Culture Series". With extensive body modifications forcefields etc. In some ways the technology is so advanced it falls into the background and it becomes about politics and the struggle of individual humans to live their lives. I like this. It makes this far flung future feel more real.

title: She Who Became the Sun
author: Shelley Parker-Chan
body: This was an enjoyable read though I found the genre mix-up a little confusing. I liked the historical fiction and delving into the world of 14th century China. The swapping of genders of the main protagonist was interesting and made the novel an intersting study on identify and reality. I found the "magic" elements a bit too much for a novel that is rooted so clearly in history. The "manadate of heaven" magic tipped this novel slightly further into fantasy terratory. In terms of style I found it easily readable and well paced. The imagry was strong and the emotional depth of the key protagonists was well drawn. I also liked the moral ambiguity of all the characters we grow to love. All are guilty of ruthlessness to achieve their ends using destiny as an inescapble trap to do terrible things.

title: Xenocide
author: Orson Scott Card
body: I was surprised at how much I enjoyed this. Interesting exploration on the nature of humanity, consciousness and the bonds that unite people. Provided an interesting study of the nature of authority and the metaphysics of religion.

title: The new life
author: Tom Crewe
body:
#bookclub

Beautiful and erotically charged. Paints London and the people of Victorian London vividly. The wife of John / Johnny is a character I would like to have seen more of. In a way she suffers as much as anyone but has no agency. She is a victim of sexism and of the law more than Johnny. And it is heavily implied that she is rapped by Johnny. The sex scenes are beautifully and erotically drawn showing male sexuality in a raw and tender way. I would have liked more of this treatment for the female characters especially Mrs Ellis and her partner. I haven’t read a better portrayal of gay male lust and sexuality.

title: The fifth season
author: N.K. Jemisin
body: This was a Fantasy novel of surprising complexity. It was only at the end that I realised that the three lead female chracters were the same person (Damaya) at diffierent times. This created a really full and in depth understanding of her characterisation. The enslavement of the Rogas was a very good theme. Jemisin's treatment of Gay, Bi and Transgender characters and non-standard relationships was subtly even beautifuly done. I found the third person present tense writting style difficult to understand at first but got used to it. Once the narator's identify is made clear - the Stone Eater who has been following the Damaya it made more sense. Though I find novels written in the present tense difficult in general and less compelling than others. However, the plot, pacing, dialogue and chracterisation allowed me to see through this. The world is not so brilliantly drawn as other fantasy series. However, the focus on chracterisation more than makes up for it.

title: The children of the mind
author: Orson Scott Card
body: Enjoyable read but lost some of the philosophical grounding of the first three novels. Still a great adventure story.

title: The Obelisk Gate
author: N.K. Jemisin
body:
This very much felt fluke a second book in the trilogy. More characters are introduced and the plot widens out. Our characters start to move independently and some threads start to come together. Jemisin has mastered her use of the present tense third person or I am just getting used to it. The narrative structure was not as innovate. The three parallel stories of three character being resolved into the story of one character at three different times was absent. A hard trick to pull off again in a sequel. That did make the Fifth Season feel very novel. The ending of The Obelisk Gate felt more melancholy than the ending of The Fifth Season.

It was a compelling read and I’m looking forward to completing the trilogy.

title: Last argument of kings
author: Joe Abercrombie
body: Delightfully dark. No character is good or evil. Some are just more evil than others. The characters are delightfully drawn with perhaps the exception of Black Dow. I look forward to reading more books by Joe Abercrombie.

title: Ender in Exile
author: Orson Scott Card
body: I was very disappointed by this novel. I did not feel, in context of the rest of the Ender books, that this story needed telling, or at least not in the way it was. The story was pre-occupied with a power dynamic between the captain of the colony ship and Ender. The wider development of Ender was not made clear. The relationship between his sister and Ender was not resolved or fully drawn. The confrontation at the end with the colonist who thought he was Achilles son didn't feel necessary. It felt like a string of events, vignettes, that didn't come together into an overall arc. As such I found I was not emotionally drawn into the story as I have been with the other books.

title: Best served cold
author: Joe Abercrombie
body:
I expected another deeply dark tale from Abercrombie and this was delivered. I wasn't sure what to expect.

Revenge was the clear driving force through the book and I found this motivation for the characters a little shallow. And it made the book feel a bit like a formulaic heist story. Gather a crew, go through a series of missions. However, the story did allow for focus and introspection and an understanding that revenge is often hollow. The final act of Monza releasing Shivers shows that people can change.

Watching Shivers become harder, abandoning his goal of becoming a better man felt tragic. The obvious parallel between him and the Bloody Nine became more and more apparent and at times I felt that his character wasn't sufficiently distinguished from the Bloody Nine.

The pacing worked well overall but I felt the lines of the plot were a predictable. There are 7 people to kill and so the story was laid out from the beginning.

The book felt a little pointless. It enables another nation of the First Law world to be drawn a little better, but the story felt pointless. It didn't weave into the arc of the universe or add any real depth to the characters and politics of the first law series.

title: Bands of mourning
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable great adventure. Felt the characters were well drawn however it was a little predictable and felt a bit hackneyed. But the world is amazing and the lore impressive.

title: A boy's own story
author: Edmund White
body: This was a deeply moving novel. I saw myself reflected in the the protagonist clearly. I found the timeline in the story interesting. It jumped around quite a bit reflecting back and forward in time. White in his post-script puts this style down to his drinking at the time of writting. I found it added to the story and feeling of authenticity as it felt like a friend telling you their life story. They jump around, skip ahead and loop back just in this way. White's prose is excellent, crisp and beautifully described. The similes are rich and I never felt frustrated by the pace and length of time taken to describe the world. A real joy to read.

title: Wind and truth
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable conclusion without the moral weight and grace of the first few books. The twist wasn’t quite twisty enough however a good tale. The world building was incredible. Will read the next story ark.

title: The rise of Endymion
author: Dan Simmons
body: Extremely good. I guessed that Raul would be the father. Don’t see A. Bettick as the observer. Excellent resolution to the story. Mixes metaphysics and theology beautifully. A hymn to humanism and the hope of a better future.

title: The blade itself
author: Joe Abercrombie
body: Extremely enjoyable. Dark bloody but full of humor. The characters and world are vivid and the plot interesting. This really was a great opening.

title: Speaker for the dead
author: Orson Scott Card
body: Interesting follow up to Enders Game. Explores morality and a good character study. How Ended became so unbelievably good at reading people isn’t clear. Liked the description of a completely different life cycle and what that means for morality.

title: Endymion
author: Dan Simmons
body: I loved this book. I struggled with the first two Hyperion books but this one was a joy to read. Mysterious and well paced. The story had me hooked. I look forward to reading the next!

title: Edge Dancer
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable little novel. Lift is a great character and so enjoyable to read.

title: Dawnshard
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable short book expanding the universe and lore of the Stormlight Archive series.

title: Before they are hanged
author: Joe Abercrombie
body: Amazing how Abercrombie makes a torturer a sympathetic character. Another thoroughly good read. Incredible character work a slow but building plot. Where is it all going to go.

title: Ultra-processed people
author: Chris van Tulleken
body:
#bookclub

Very readable. Point is very labored at times. Made me reconsider how I eat.

title: Too like the lightning
author: Ada Palmer
body: Not sure what to make of this one. Challenging. At times sound of hard to follow and the narrator style difficult to follow. The different factions “Hives” hard to keep straight. Not sure what the Bash system adds to the narrative. They are families. Extended families.

title: The alloy of Law
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable return to the world of Mistborn. I enjoy the Victorian line works and the new characters. Tight not group and close focus on three characters.

title: The Darkness That Comes Before
author: R. Scott Bakker
body:
Did not finish

Couldn’t get into this. Gave up. I found the names confusing and the lore hard to follow. Couldn’t get a feel for the characters or the world.

title: Shadows of self
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable adventure. More of the comers opening up. Nothing ground breaking on the previous novels.

title: Prince of thorns
author: Mark Lawrence
body: Very enjoyable. Dark and twisted and visceral. You can hear and smell the clash and stink of battle. The stench of the brothers leaps off the page. Pacing excellent descriptions vividly drawn. Questions on the origin of the world. Our world a 1,000 years after nuclear war?

title: Pandora’s star
author: Peter F. Hamilton
body: The first part of the story. We are left with a literal cliff hanger as Ozzie, Orion and their alien companion fall of the edge of a disk world and the prime invasion os happening and key players are realizing the starter os real. The only complaint with the star flyer idea is that it has to be real to be included in the plot. Part of me wonders if the SI (Sentient Intelligence) is the Starflyer, I suspect this even more seeing that the. Ext book is called Judas Unchained . Will read the next one right away to continue the story.

title: Judas unchained
author: Peter F. Hamilton
body: So the SI wasn’t the starflyer! Enjoyed a lot. I found the Silken and Izzie subplot unnecessary it didn’t carry the story forward and had only a small point towards the plot. Ozzie has humanities conscience could have got in to the plot elsewhere. I enjoyed the style and the epic world building. However the characterization is weak. Apart from Melanie who I felt I began to understand and see develop. Other characters felt one dimensional.

title: Enders game
author: Orson Scott Card
body: One of the best books I have read in a very long time. The twist at the end was I suppose, in hindsight, inevitable but I did t see it. Ended is so beautifully drawn. The battle school so well imagined. The story felt vast even though it was short and set in a very small space.

title: Wrath
author: John Gwynne
body: Good if a little predictable. Seemed a little random who Gwynne chose to live and to die. But also there were predictable events. That Gar would die, that Asroth would come into the world. That Corban would reject power. Enjoyed. Will read more from this author.

title: The well of ascension
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Entertaining. Good twist at the end. Good pace. Will likely read the next one.

title: The hero of ages
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Reverent twist at the end of full of action, drama and pathos. Though I didn’t engage with the characters as much as I have with other books.

title: Ruin
author: John Gwynne
body: Enjoyable read. Fast paced with lots of action. Though the pacing makes the character work less strong and the world building not as good as other novels. Almost every scene is a battle or clash. No quiet moments at all.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
body: Uplifting exciting and optimistic. A brilliant and engaging story of shared humanity and compassion. I enjoyed the first person style.

title: Disquiet Gods
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: Greatly enjoyed. The world (universe) building is fantastic. The terror and horror of Vorgossos feels very real. The Christian theology comes over very strongly. Feels a bit heavy handed. The hybrid of fantasy and sci fi works really well.

title: Valour
author: John Gwynne
body: Hugely enjoyable. Fast paced and great characterization. There is a good exploration of characters. They are not all good or all bad. Motivations explored. Clearly influenced by Tolkien and Robert Jordan. But original.

title: The sunlit man
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyable sci-fi fantasy fusion.

title: The final empire
author: Brandon Sanderson
body:
Starts like a heist story as a crew are brought together but expands to be so much more. Who is this immortal “Lord Ruler”

Highly enjoyable. Not as good as the storm light archive books but the adventure and characterization was good. I did find some of the characters a little shallow. Ellen’s could have been deepened. Why did he think differently to other nobles what happened in his past? But there will be other books to learn more about him.

title: Malice
author: John Gwynne
body: Really enjoyed this. Fast paced and vivid characters. Loved the animal companion. Lines the story telling from different perspectives. Found myself more drawn into Corban’s plot than others and caring about him more. Reminded me of how Robert Jordan used different character perspectives in the wheel of time.

title: Lessons on Chemistry
author: Bonnie Garmus
body:
#bookclub

Enjoyable family saga. Elizabeth Zott the protagonist did not feel real. She is too perfect. She represents the ideal modern female. Independent and courageous.

Book gives a good view of mid century sexism and the challenges women face.

I found the portrait of Zott as too perfect almost decision itself. She never shouted at Mad, never bullied, never sort revenge. She sets a standard that’s impossible to reach.

title: Yellowface
author: Rebecca F Kuang
body:
#bookclub

This was a challenging read. It felt like an extended anxiety nightmare. The protagonist is entirely unlikeable. At points I found myself rooting for her if only so her pain would end.

Her predicament is entirely self inflicted she makes a series of horrible judgements and entirely selfish choices justified by what she thinks she feels she deserves.

The novel describes how the publishing industry works, ruthlessly. How it forces authors to compete and how high the stakes are.

Juni implicitly accepts her privilege and sees Asian authors success as a slight against her.

Question the book raises is are all authors magpies steeling the nuggets and content from other peoples lives. Where does inspiration end and theft or plagiarism begin?

title: The prisoner of heaven
author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
body: Another enigmatic novel by Zafón I don’t really know what to make of it except incredibly atmospheric gives a real view of Franco Spain and Barcelona of the 30s 40s and 50s. The plot is dark but simple Daniel unsure of who his father really is learn that his father is not his father or maybe Heiz we learn more about Martin’s time in the prison in Barcelona and how Fermin to meet him. I enjoyed the book and read it with these, but I did not feel compelled to pick it up and continue reading it.

title: Red Rising
author: Pierce Brown
body: Real page turner. I’m enjoying the universe that is being built. Very much a coming of age story.

title: Morning star
author: Pierce Brown
body: Third in the story. I grew frustrated towards the end that the same cycle of capture and defeat would continue. Brown had me deceived. Loved the twist at the end. Don’t think I will rush to read the next volume. But will get to them I’m sure.

title: Golden Son
author: Pierce Brown
body: Expands on the u inverse of the finally irst novel. We see Darrow grow up a bit and struggle through a number of challenges. Very entertaining and couldn’t put it down. I wounded if they revealed Darrow’s secret of his “Red” origins too soon. Will have to wait to find out.

title: Dissolution
author: C.J. Sansom
body: Very enjoyable read. Real page turner and well researched. Look forward to reading another.

title: Dark Fire
author: C.J. Sansom
body: Enjoyable historical thriller. This novel lacked the dark menace of the first. But was still an enjoyable page turner.

title: Shōgun
author: James Clavell
body: Vivid recreation of late medieval (Elizabethan Japan) the characters well drawn. The story engaging. Sometimes find it difficult to follow the change in perspective as the story switches point of view between characters.

title: Rythym of War
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Hugely enjoyable with a great deal of character and plot development. Aeolian still feels very 1 dimensional to me. All other characters have failings, flaws but Adolin to perfect. Was a bit too long.

title: Kingdoms of death
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: Horror pain adventure loss this was a dark but violently told tale. Where this goes in the story or why this needed to happen I do not know but I enjoyed the journey!

title: Ashes of Man
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: Wow. Read this in one weekend. Fantastic space opera. Fast paced and full of pathos. At times I was close to tears. My pulse high as I read the action. Great fun. But what to read next.

title: Words of radiance
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Really enjoyed this second installment of the Stormlight archive. The characters are starting to come together. However I felt the ending scenes were a little cheesy. Especially between Dalinar & Shallan. The nature of the universe is starting to come clear.

title: The way of kings
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Massively enjoyed this fantasy novel. Characters are well drawn and it keeps you guessing. So many cliffhangers and questions at the end. The magic isn’t so well drawn and the nature of storm light feels like it needs some more explanation. I look forward to reading the next one!

title: The demon in white
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: Another romping adventure. The tale is linking fantasy and sci-fi together well. The battle the threat all great. This one feels like a middle book which I suppose it is!

title: Oathbringer
author: Brandon Sanderson
body: Enjoyed this one. Same wonderful battle scenes. But some issues with the pacing and plot. How did they get the singers out of Urithu? Looking forward to the final two books

title: Howling Dark
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: Really enjoyed this book the second in the sun eater series. The familiar set of characters is here with an expanded set. The threat of Vogossos, the terror of battle and the conflict behind Hadrian’s actions are all really well drawn. Excited to read the next one.

title: The secret speech
author: Tom Rob Smith
body: Another great thriller by Tom Rob Smith. Great romp, lots of blood and gore. The plot stretched credulity but then they do say “stranger then Fiction”. The story continues to follow Leo & Raisa as they try to build their lives with their adopted daughters in post Stalin USSR.

title: The drawing of the three
author: Stephen King
body: Very much enjoyed. Not like a fantasy I’ve read before. Roland, Eddie and Odetta/Detta/Samantha are a great combination. The interplay between them masterfully drawn. The horror real.

title: The Gun Slinger
author: Stephen King
body: Very much tell this was written by a 19 year old. The book felt like one long introductory chapter which I guess it is. So many unanswered questions about this world. Looking forward to reading more.

title: Surface detail
author: Ian M Banks
body: Penultimate culture series novel and this sees the culture at its best. Lots of exciting politics, space battles and mischievous minds all working together at cross purposes with the humans getting caught in between.

title: Empire of Silence
author: Christopher Ruocchio
body: The world is heavily influenced by Dune. With an emperor, noble class, monopolies guilds, a ban on AI with trained humans filling the place of computers. However the universe is richer and the story more relatable. Some of the technology is inspired by “the culture” series. Eg. neural nets. I look forward to reading the next one.

title: Alien Clay
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
body: Very entertaining but formulaic. The commandant was a one dimensional antagonist.

title: Hyperion Cantos book 2 the fall of Hyperion
author: Dan Simmons
body: Overly long and mystical. Enjoyable in parts. Might consider reading more of the series.

title: Child 44
author: Tom Rob Smith
body: Great adventure and detective model set in Stalin’s Russia where there can be no crime.

title: Ice people
author: Tom Rob Smith
body: Cinematic. Begging for a sequel. Clearly inspired by the three body problem

title: Hyperion Cantos Book 1
author: Daniel Simmons
body: Hard SiFi but interesting. Book clearly the first in a series. Who / what is the Shrike. What is the AI core up to?

title: City of Thieves
author: David Benioff
body: Enjoyed this but very dark. Some of the characters lacked depth. But a great grim adventure

title: The Angel's Game
author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
body: Psychological thriller with ambiguous ending. Second in the graveyard of forgotten books series.

title: Reasons Not to Worry: How to be Stoic in chaotic times
author: Brigid Delaney
body: A very good and practical guide to stoicism. I didn’t like some of the writing style. Referring to “vibes”. Makes me want to read more on the subject.

title: Great expectations
author: Charles Dickens
body: Never read Dickens before. Very good story told from the fist person perspective. Didn’t expect the ending to be quite so melancholy. Dickens created larger than life characters they jump from the page. Some ridiculous. Not sure if they are meant to be believed or are characters.
